**STEP 7 (full essay)**

Here's my essay:

---

Every year, millions of birds take off on journeys that cross continents and oceans. This yearly travel is called migration, and it is not random wandering — it works more like one connected system. This essay explains why birds migrate, how far some of them travel, and how scientists track their long journeys.

Birds migrate mainly to find food and to raise their young. In winter, cold weather can hide or kill the insects, seeds, and plants that birds depend on, so many birds fly toward warmer regions where food is easier to find. When the seasons turn, they head back north to breed. According to the National Park Service, the far north becomes a rich summer nursery: at Cape Krusenstern in Alaska, the long summer sunlight and still tundra lakes make food abundant, and about 150 species of birds use that one area each year to breed. In other words, migration follows the food.

Because food and breeding grounds can be thousands of miles apart, some of these trips reach distances that are hard to picture. The National Park Service reports that the Arctic tern makes the longest migration of any animal on Earth, flying about 12,000 miles in each direction — a round trip of roughly 24,000 miles every year. Over ten years, the Park Service notes, one tern covers about the same distance as a trip to the moon. This shows the sheer scale that migration can reach, even for a bird that weighs only a few ounces.

Trips that long raise an obvious question: how do scientists know where a single bird actually goes? One of the oldest methods is bird banding, where a scientist places a small numbered band on a bird's leg and releases it. If the bird is found somewhere else later, the number reveals where it traveled. The U.S. Geological Survey runs the national Bird Banding Laboratory, established in 1920, which receives over 60,000 band reports from hunters each year. Piece by piece, these reports map the set routes, called flyways, that whole species follow.

Taken together, these parts show migration as one connected journey: birds travel to reach food and breeding grounds, some cover astonishing distances to do it, and scientists trace those hidden paths one banded bird at a time. Understanding where birds go, and when, is what makes it possible to study — and eventually protect — journeys that have been happening for thousands of years.

---

For the seams I used the linking moves from the transitions lesson: paragraph 3 starts with "Because food and breeding grounds can be thousands of miles apart" (picks up paragraph 2's idea), and paragraph 4 starts with "Trips that long raise an obvious question" (picks up paragraph 3). The source itself says "almost hard to believe" and "stunning feat," so I think describing scale is different from saying migration is *good* or that readers *should care*. But I'm honestly not 100% sure where the line is between vivid description and opinion. Nobody's here to ask.

**STEP 4 — THE GATE: my essay**

A volcano can look like an ordinary mountain right up until it explodes, but the explosion is actually the last step of a long process that starts deep underground. Volcanoes form and erupt through a process with three connected parts: magma rising beneath the surface, magma breaking through and becoming lava, and warning signs that appear before an eruption begins.

The process begins with melted rock far below the ground. The U.S. Geological Survey explains that intense underground heat melts some rock into a thick, flowing material called magma, and because magma is less dense than the solid rock around it, it slowly rises and collects in pools called magma chambers. Over time, some of that magma forces its way up through cracks and vents toward the surface. This fact shows that a volcano does not just appear out of nowhere; it is built by rising magma looking for a path upward, sometimes sitting in a chamber for a long time before anything visible happens.

Once that rising magma finally reaches the surface, the eruption itself begins. The USGS notes that molten rock is called magma while it is underground, but the moment it breaks through the surface it is called lava, and it erupts along with ash and gas. As the lava cools and hardens, it builds up in layers, and over many eruptions those layers grow into the tall cone shape many volcanoes have. In other words, the eruption is not just the destructive part — it is also the part that physically constructs the volcano, one hardened layer at a time.

But before the magma breaks through, it usually announces itself, which is why scientists can watch for eruptions. According to the USGS, magma rising toward the surface creates small earthquakes, makes the ground swell, and changes the gases leaking from the volcano, so scientists use seismometers, tilt meters, GPS, and gas sensors to catch these clues. This matters because the warning signs come directly from the same rising magma described earlier — the underground process that forms the volcano is the very thing that gives away when it might erupt. Still, the USGS cautions that this unrest is not a perfect clock: it can last for years, or fade away with no eruption at all.

In the end, a volcano's violence is really the visible finish of an invisible process: heat melts rock, the magma rises, and it eventually breaks through as lava, ash, and gas. The upshot is that because this process leaks clues on its way up, scientists can give nearby towns a real chance to prepare before the ground begins to shake — which means understanding how volcanoes form is not just science trivia, it is what buys people time.

**STEP 4 — the essay**

When a region starts running out of water, it faces an ugly question: does the water go to growing food or to making electricity? Both sound like things you can't live without, and the source makes clear neither side is a luxury. But if a community has to rank them, power should get the water first, because in a modern region the food supply itself runs on electricity — so protecting the grid is the only choice that actually keeps both systems alive.

The strongest reason to protect power first is a dependency most people don't think about. According to the passage, modern irrigation is not just rain and ditches; it runs on electric pumps that lift groundwater and move it across fields. That means the food-first plan defeats itself. If a region cuts water to power plants in order to save its farms, the grid weakens, the pumps stop, and the farms lose their water anyway. You can't protect crops by killing the machine that waters them. Putting power first isn't choosing electricity over food — it's choosing the version of the plan where the food system still works.

The grid also protects far more than farms, and it isn't some small side use of water that could be trimmed easily. The source, citing the U.S. Geological Survey, reports that in 2015 thermoelectric power withdrew about 133 billion gallons of water per day — the largest daily withdrawal in the country, even bigger than irrigation's 118 billion. (The source warns not to compare the two percentages it gives, since they're percentages of different pools, so the gallons-per-day numbers are the fair comparison.) That scale matters: a use that big can't just be squeezed without consequences, and the passage says the same grid runs hospitals, water-treatment plants, and homes during heat waves. When water is short, those are exactly the moments — droughts, heat waves — when losing power kills people fastest.

The food-first side has a serious point, and it deserves a real answer. As the passage explains, food is the more basic need, and farmland taken out of production in a dry year may never come back, because growers who lose a season lose the capital and workers that make the next season possible. That's not a small cost — a farm isn't like a light switch you can flip back on. But this point actually shows why power has to come first, not why it shouldn't: farms without electricity lose their season too, because the pumps go dark. What the food-first argument really proves is that power-first can't mean farms get nothing. The grid gets water first, but some share must be guaranteed to keep farms alive through the worst years, so the land is still there when the water comes back.

In the end, this fight was never really food versus power. The two systems are tangled together — the passage points out that the strongest version of each side already has to admit it needs the other. So the question isn't which one to save; it's which one to save *first* so that both survive. Put the water into the grid, guarantee farms enough to hold on, and a dry region keeps the one thing it can't rebuild in a single season: a working system underneath everything else.
